question_text: Rose (Rosa spp.) is often called the 'Queen of Flowers' and holds the position of the most important commercial cut flower in the world — which of the following is the most commercially important rose class used for long-stemmed cut flower production in international markets?

options.text: Polyantha — cluster-flowered short bushes
Hybrid Tea — large single flowers on long straight stems
Miniature — dwarf plants under 30 cm height
Climber — long-caned with overhead training

explanation: Hybrid Tea roses (HT) are the most important commercial class for cut flower production globally. They produce large (8–15 cm diameter), high-centred, single blooms on long straight stems (60–80 cm), making them ideal for bouquets and floral arrangements. Varieties like First Prize, Gladiator, Super Star, Montezuma, and Pusa Gaurav are widely grown. Floribunda roses (cluster-flowered) are used for garden display. Polyantha, miniature, and climber roses are used as bedding, potted, and landscape plants respectively — not for premium cut flower trade.

elimination_hints.hint: Miniatures are too short (under 30 cm) for cut flower stems.
Polyantha clusters lack the single large centred blooms needed for premium bouquets.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Rose propagation for commercial plantations is done primarily by budding since seed-grown plants produce highly variable progeny — which specific budding method is most widely used in Indian commercial rose nurseries?

options.text: Patch budding in May–June on young seedling rootstocks
T-budding (shield budding) on one-year-old Rosa indica or Rosa multiflora rootstocks during active growth
Chip budding in midwinter on dormant seedling rootstocks
Forkert budding in monsoon on matured bark of rootstock plants

explanation: T-budding (shield budding) is the standard commercial method for rose propagation in Indian nurseries. A T-shaped cut is made on the bark of one-year-old rootstock (Rosa indica 'Odorata' or Rosa multiflora) during the active growth period (January–March in plains), and a single bud (shield) with a small piece of bark is slipped under the loosened bark of the T-cut. Success rate is 85–95% when performed skilfully. Rosa indica is preferred for its wide adaptability, while Rosa multiflora suits cooler climates. Chip budding is used occasionally; patch and Forkert budding are used for other crops.

elimination_hints.hint: Patch and Forkert budding are more common in tropical fruits like mango.
Chip budding in midwinter has much lower success rates in rose.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Marigold (Tagetes spp.) is one of India's most economically important loose flower crops used in garlands, religious offerings, and xanthophyll pigment extraction — which species is commonly known as African marigold and produces large globular flowers used for commercial loose flower trade?

options.text: Tagetes patula (French marigold with smaller bicoloured flowers)
Tagetes erecta (African marigold with large yellow-orange globular flowers)
Tagetes lucida (Mexican mint marigold used as a culinary herb)
Tagetes minuta (tall stink marigold used for essential oil extraction)

explanation: Tagetes erecta is the African marigold (also called American marigold) characterised by large globular flower heads (5–10 cm diameter), yellow to deep orange colour, and tall plant height (60–120 cm). It is the dominant commercial marigold in India for loose flower production, garland making, and xanthophyll extraction (for poultry feed colouring). Popular varieties include Pusa Narangi Gainda (orange), Pusa Basanti Gainda (yellow), and Seracole. Tagetes patula is French marigold (smaller bicoloured flowers used for bedding and nematode suppression); T. minuta is tall stink marigold for essential oil; T. lucida is a culinary herb.

elimination_hints.hint: French marigold (T. patula) produces smaller bicoloured blooms — unsuitable for garland trade.
T. minuta and T. lucida are specialty species not used for loose flower markets.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Jasmine (Jasminum spp.) is culturally and commercially significant in South India with Madurai Malli enjoying a Geographical Indication (GI) tag — which species is the source of the famous Madurai Malli that also dominates the 'Gundumalli' scented jasmine trade?

options.text: Jasminum grandiflorum (Spanish jasmine / Jati)
Jasminum sambac (Arabian jasmine / Mullai / Gundumalli)
Jasminum auriculatum (Juhi / needle-flowered jasmine)
Jasminum multiflorum (Kundo / downy jasmine)

explanation: Jasminum sambac (Arabian jasmine) is locally known as Mullai (Tamil) or Gundumalli, and is the source of the famous Madurai Malli, which received the GI tag in 2013. Its small white highly fragrant flowers are the most prized in South Indian loose flower trade, especially Tamil Nadu, where women adorn their hair with fresh jasmine daily. Jasminum grandiflorum (Jati) is used primarily for extracting jasmine oil (concrete and absolute) for perfumery. J. auriculatum (Juhi) is another scented species. J. multiflorum is less commercially important. Tamil Nadu is the leading jasmine state.

elimination_hints.hint: Jasminum grandiflorum is mainly for oil extraction, not fresh flower trade.
'Mullai' directly corresponds to Jasminum sambac in Tamil horticulture vocabulary.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Tuberose (Polianthes tuberosa) is a popular cut flower and loose flower crop valued for its strong fragrance and long vase life — which of the following is the main mode of propagation used in commercial tuberose cultivation?

options.text: Seed propagation through dry seeds sown in nursery beds
Bulbs weighing 20–30 grams planted in well-prepared soil at 20×20 cm spacing
Hardwood cuttings treated with IBA rooting hormone
Tissue culture micro-propagation as the only commercial method

explanation: Tuberose is commercially propagated through bulbs weighing 20–30 g planted at 20×20 cm spacing (giving 2.5 lakh bulbs/ha) in well-prepared raised beds. Bulbs are planted with 2–3 cm soil cover, and spike production begins in 90–120 days. Seeds are rarely used commercially due to slow multiplication and hybrid progeny. Hardwood cuttings are irrelevant for a bulbous plant. Tissue culture is used for virus-free planting material production but not as the primary field-scale propagation method. Popular varieties include Shringar (Single), Suvasini (Double), Rajat Rekha, and Swarna Rekha.

elimination_hints.hint: Tuberose is a classical bulbous plant — hardwood cuttings don't apply.
Seeds yield highly variable progeny unsuitable for commercial production.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Gladiolus (Gladiolus grandiflorus) is a popular cut flower called 'Sword Lily' due to its characteristic long spike inflorescence with multiple florets — which plant organ serves as the primary propagule used in commercial gladiolus cultivation?

options.text: Corm — solid underground stem with nodes and scaly leaves
Bulb — true bulb with fleshy concentric scales
Rhizome — horizontal underground stem growing along soil surface
Tuber — swollen underground stem lacking nodes and scales

explanation: Gladiolus is propagated through corms — solid, swollen underground stems with distinct nodes and scaly leaves, differentiating them from true bulbs (with fleshy concentric scales like onion). Corms weighing 20–40 g (above 5 cm circumference) are planted at 15–20 cm spacing in raised beds during October–December in North India. New corms and small cormels form at the base of the old corm; these are lifted after harvest, cured, and stored for the next season. Popular varieties include White Prosperity, Friendship (pink), Jacksonville Gold (yellow), and IARI's Suchitra and Swarnima.

elimination_hints.hint: True bulbs (with concentric scales) are found in onion, lily, tulip — not gladiolus.
Rhizomes (ginger, canna) grow horizontally — gladiolus corm is vertical.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Chrysanthemum (Chrysanthemum morifolium), often called the 'Queen of the East', is a photoperiod-sensitive flower crop widely used for manipulation of flowering time in commercial cultivation — which category of photoperiodic response does chrysanthemum belong to?

options.text: Long-day plant requiring more than 14 hours of daylight for flowering
Short-day plant requiring less than 12 hours of daylight (12–13 hours critical photoperiod) for flower bud initiation
Day-neutral plant that flowers irrespective of photoperiod conditions
Intermediate-day plant requiring 13–14 hours of daylight for flowering

explanation: Chrysanthemum is a classical short-day plant (SDP) — it requires day length shorter than the critical photoperiod (12–13 hours depending on variety) for flower bud initiation. Commercial growers exploit this by using artificial light (night-break with 60–100 lux incandescent lamps for 3–5 hours at midnight) during short-day seasons to keep plants vegetative for longer stems, then withdrawing light to induce flowering on demand. This enables year-round production. Short-day behaviour also explains natural autumn/winter flowering in chrysanthemum. Long-day plants include dianthus and gaillardia; day-neutral includes rose.

elimination_hints.hint: The characteristic autumn/winter natural flowering indicates short-day response.
Commercial night-break lighting would have no effect on day-neutral plants.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Floricultural crops are classified based on end use and market destination which determines their production practices and post-harvest handling — which of the following correctly identifies the main commercial classification categories of flowers in India?

options.text: Only cut flowers and loose flowers — two categories covering entire commerce
Cut flowers, loose flowers, bedding plants, dried flowers, and cut foliage as distinct categories
Only indoor plants and outdoor plants based on cultivation environment
Only perennial and annual flowers based on plant life cycle duration

explanation: Indian floriculture is commercially classified into: (1) Cut flowers — stems with flowers sold for bouquets and arrangements (rose, gerbera, carnation, gladiolus); (2) Loose flowers — flower heads without stems for garlands and religious offerings (marigold, jasmine, chrysanthemum, tuberose); (3) Bedding/potted plants — for landscape and indoor decor; (4) Dried flowers — for exports and long-lasting arrangements (statice, helichrysum); (5) Cut foliage — greenery used in arrangements (asparagus, leatherleaf fern, cordyline). Each category has distinct agronomic practices, harvest timing, and market channels.

elimination_hints.hint: A is oversimplified — ignores major categories like potted plants and dried flowers.
C and D are plant science classifications, not commercial flower trade classifications.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: India's floriculture sector has grown substantially and the country now ranks as a significant global player — which Indian state holds the top position in flower production including both loose and cut flowers?

options.text: Tamil Nadu (strong in jasmine, marigold, and crossandra)
Karnataka (diverse flower base including rose, chrysanthemum, jasmine)
Maharashtra (major marigold and rose producer)
West Bengal (tuberose and marigold producer)

explanation: Tamil Nadu is India's largest flower producing state, contributing approximately 12–15% of national output, driven by its dominant loose flower production — especially jasmine (Madurai Malli), marigold, chrysanthemum (samanthi), crossandra (kanakambaram), and nerium. Karnataka ranks second with a strong base in cut flowers (rose, gerbera, carnation) especially from Bangalore region where the International Flower Auction Bangalore (IFAB) operates. Other major states include West Bengal (tuberose), Maharashtra (rose, marigold), and Andhra Pradesh. India is the world's second-largest flower producer after China, but a relatively small exporter compared to Netherlands/Kenya.

elimination_hints.hint: Tamil Nadu's dominance in loose flowers (jasmine, marigold) drives total production leadership.
Karnataka leads in cut flowers and exports but TN leads in total output.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: The Dutch auction system, practised extensively at Aalsmeer (Netherlands) and at the International Flower Auction Bangalore (IFAB), is characterised by a specific price discovery mechanism unique to commodity markets — what is the key feature of the Dutch auction?

options.text: Price starts low and increases progressively until the final bidder matches the seller's reserve price
Price starts high and decreases continuously through a clock mechanism until the first buyer presses a button to accept
Sealed written bids are submitted and the highest bidder wins the lot at the second-highest price
Multiple auctioneers compete simultaneously with separate fixed prices for each lot

explanation: The Dutch auction is a 'descending price' auction where the auctioneer starts with a very high price and the displayed price on an electronic clock decreases continuously. The first buyer to press their button 'stops the clock' and wins the entire lot at that price. This system is extremely efficient for perishable commodities like flowers, as it allows rapid price discovery for hundreds of lots per hour. Aalsmeer Flower Auction in the Netherlands (the world's largest) and Bangalore's IFAB use this system. Option A describes English auction; option C describes Vickrey auction; option D is a unrelated hypothetical.

elimination_hints.hint: English auction (A) starts low and bids upward — opposite of Dutch auction.
Sealed-bid (C) lacks the distinctive 'clock' mechanism that defines Dutch auction.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each flower crop with the plant organ or material used as its primary commercial propagule in Indian floriculture, which is critical for genetic uniformity and large-scale production.
column_b: Corms — solid underground stems with nodes lifted and cured after harvest for next season
Bulbs of 20–30 g weight planted at 20×20 cm spacing in raised beds
T-budding on Rosa indica or Rosa multiflora rootstocks during active growth
Seed-raised transplants from nursery beds, sown 30–40 days before main field planting

correct_mapping.Rose: T-budding on Rosa indica or Rosa multiflora rootstocks during active growth
correct_mapping.Marigold: Seed-raised transplants from nursery beds, sown 30–40 days before main field planting
correct_mapping.Tuberose: Bulbs of 20–30 g weight planted at 20×20 cm spacing in raised beds
correct_mapping.Gladiolus: Corms — solid underground stems with nodes lifted and cured after harvest for next season

options.text: Rose→T-budding; Marigold→Seed transplants; Tuberose→Bulbs; Gladiolus→Corms
Rose→Seed transplants; Marigold→Corms; Tuberose→T-budding; Gladiolus→Bulbs
Rose→Corms; Marigold→Bulbs; Tuberose→Seed transplants; Gladiolus→T-budding
Rose→Bulbs; Marigold→T-budding; Tuberose→Corms; Gladiolus→Seed transplants

explanation: Rose is propagated by T-budding (shield budding) on one-year-old Rosa indica or Rosa multiflora rootstocks because seed-grown roses give highly variable progeny. Marigold is raised from seed — nursery seedlings are transplanted at 30–40 days to main field. Tuberose is propagated through bulbs (20–30 g) planted at 20×20 cm in raised beds. Gladiolus is propagated by corms (solid underground stems — not true bulbs) harvested, cured, and stored for next season. These distinctions are commercially and botanically important for each crop.

elimination_hints.hint: Marigold is the only seed-raised annual in this list — simple and direct.
T-budding is uniquely associated with perennial woody roses, not herbaceous flowers.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each popular flower variety with its correct crop species and distinguishing feature used for identification in commercial Indian floriculture.
column_b: Rose — hybrid tea with large deep pink blooms on long straight stems, AARS winner
Gladiolus — pure white florets on tall spikes, highly popular for cut flower trade
Tuberose — Single type with long spike and fragrant white flowers for loose and cut trade
Marigold — African type with orange globular flowers widely used for garland making

correct_mapping.Pusa Narangi Gainda: Marigold — African type with orange globular flowers widely used for garland making
correct_mapping.First Prize: Rose — hybrid tea with large deep pink blooms on long straight stems, AARS winner
correct_mapping.White Prosperity: Gladiolus — pure white florets on tall spikes, highly popular for cut flower trade
correct_mapping.Shringar: Tuberose — Single type with long spike and fragrant white flowers for loose and cut trade

options.text: Pusa Narangi Gainda→Marigold; First Prize→Rose; White Prosperity→Gladiolus; Shringar→Tuberose
Pusa Narangi Gainda→Rose; First Prize→Tuberose; White Prosperity→Marigold; Shringar→Gladiolus
Pusa Narangi Gainda→Tuberose; First Prize→Gladiolus; White Prosperity→Rose; Shringar→Marigold
Pusa Narangi Gainda→Gladiolus; First Prize→Marigold; White Prosperity→Tuberose; Shringar→Rose

explanation: Pusa Narangi Gainda is IARI's popular orange African marigold variety widely used for loose flower garland trade. First Prize is a globally popular Hybrid Tea rose (1970 AARS winner) with large deep pink blooms. White Prosperity is one of the most widely grown gladiolus varieties with pure white florets on tall spikes. Shringar is a classic Single-type tuberose variety known for its long fragrant spikes. Each variety name often hints at its crop — 'Gainda' (marigold in Hindi), 'Prize' for rose show winners, 'White' for gladiolus colour, 'Shringar' associated with wedding-related jasmine/tuberose decoration.

elimination_hints.hint: 'Gainda' is the Hindi word for marigold — a direct language-based clue.
First Prize and Rose are both terms associated with flower show victories.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each commercial flower crop with its leading producing state in India based on current agricultural statistics and recognised production belts.
column_b: West Bengal — Nadia and Howrah districts with prominent Single and Double variety cultivation
Karnataka — Bangalore and surrounding districts dominate polyhouse cut flower production
Karnataka — significant marigold production with Bangalore, Kolar, and Chikkaballapur districts leading
Tamil Nadu — Madurai belt with GI-tagged Madurai Malli leading Indian jasmine production

correct_mapping.Jasmine: Tamil Nadu — Madurai belt with GI-tagged Madurai Malli leading Indian jasmine production
correct_mapping.Rose (cut flower): Karnataka — Bangalore and surrounding districts dominate polyhouse cut flower production
correct_mapping.Marigold (loose flower): Karnataka — significant marigold production with Bangalore, Kolar, and Chikkaballapur districts leading
correct_mapping.Tuberose (cut flower): West Bengal — Nadia and Howrah districts with prominent Single and Double variety cultivation

options.text: Jasmine→Tamil Nadu/Madurai; Rose→Karnataka/Bangalore; Marigold→Karnataka; Tuberose→West Bengal/Nadia
Jasmine→Karnataka/Bangalore; Rose→Tamil Nadu/Madurai; Marigold→West Bengal; Tuberose→Karnataka
Jasmine→West Bengal; Rose→Karnataka; Marigold→Tamil Nadu; Tuberose→Karnataka
Jasmine→Karnataka; Rose→West Bengal; Marigold→Tamil Nadu; Tuberose→Karnataka/Bangalore

explanation: Tamil Nadu's Madurai belt (Madurai Malli GI tag) is the global jasmine hub. Karnataka's Bangalore region dominates cut rose production with most polyhouse exports channelled through IFAB. Karnataka also has significant marigold acreage across Bangalore, Kolar, Chikkaballapur, and Ramanagara districts. West Bengal's Nadia and Howrah districts lead tuberose production for both cut spike and loose flower markets. These state-crop associations are consistently reported in horticultural statistics.

elimination_hints.hint: Madurai Malli and Tamil Nadu are inseparably linked — a standard exam fact.
Bangalore is globally associated with cut rose exports through IFAB.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Analyse the Assertion and Reason below regarding cold chain management for cut flower exports and choose the option that correctly describes their relationship.
assertion: Cold chain management from harvest to retail is absolutely critical for maintaining the quality and vase life of cut flowers like rose, gerbera, and carnation destined for export markets.
reason: Cut flowers continue to respire post-harvest and rapidly consume their stored carbohydrate reserves in warm conditions, leading to premature wilting, ethylene sensitivity, and loss of aesthetic quality within days.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — cold chain management is critical for cut flower export quality, especially for premium markets like Japan, Middle East, and Europe. The reason correctly explains the physiological basis: cut flowers are living organisms that continue respiring after harvest, consuming stored sugars and releasing heat. High temperatures accelerate respiration 2–3 fold for every 10°C rise (Q10 effect), deplete carbohydrates faster, increase ethylene sensitivity, and cause premature wilting. Cold chain at 2–4°C for most cut flowers slows all these processes and extends vase life. The reason directly justifies the assertion's requirement for cold chain.

elimination_hints.hint: Cold chain for cut flower exports is a non-negotiable international trade requirement.
The respiration-temperature relationship and Q10 effect are standard physiology.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate the Assertion and Reason below about photoperiodic manipulation in chrysanthemum commercial cultivation and select the option that best describes their relationship.
assertion: Commercial chrysanthemum growers use night-break lighting with low-intensity incandescent lamps for 3–5 hours around midnight during short-day seasons to delay flowering and extend vegetative growth for longer cut stems.
reason: Chrysanthemum is a short-day plant where flowering is induced by long continuous nights, and a brief light break during the dark period interrupts the critical dark phase, preventing flower bud initiation.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — night-break lighting is a standard commercial practice in chrysanthemum production, especially for year-round cut flower operations in polyhouses. The reason correctly explains the photoperiodism science: modern photoperiodism research (since Hamner and Bonner in the 1930s) established that short-day plants actually require a long uninterrupted dark period (night length is the critical factor, not day length). A light break of just a few minutes during the critical dark phase resets the phytochrome system and prevents flower bud initiation, keeping plants vegetative. Night-break is practised as 3–5 hours low-intensity light at midnight (2200–0200 hrs).

elimination_hints.hint: The 'long night' requirement of short-day plants is fundamental to plant photoperiod science.
Night-break manipulation is a textbook commercial application of photoperiodism.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below about India's position in global floriculture trade and determine whether both statements are correct and if the reason explains the assertion accurately.
assertion: Despite being the second-largest flower producer globally, India's share in world flower exports remains relatively modest at around 1–2 percent of global trade.
reason: Indian floriculture is dominated by loose flower production for domestic consumption in religious and cultural events, while global trade is dominated by export-grade cut flowers requiring strict quality standards and cold chain infrastructure.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — India ranks #2 in production but has only a small share in global flower exports; Netherlands, Kenya, Colombia, and Ecuador dominate international trade. The reason correctly explains this paradox: Indian flower production is oriented toward domestic loose flower markets (garlands, religious offerings, weddings) — high volume but lower unit value. Global trade focuses on cut flowers (rose, gerbera, carnation) demanding polyhouse production, uniform quality, and unbroken cold chain — areas where India is catching up but still lags behind established exporters. Government schemes under MIDH and APEDA are working to bridge this gap.

elimination_hints.hint: The production-export paradox of Indian floriculture is widely reported in trade data.
The structural difference between domestic loose flower and global cut flower markets is well-documented.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following operations in the correct chronological sequence for commercial rose cut flower production from planting to first harvest in a controlled polyhouse environment.
items: Plant grafted rose saplings at 30×30 cm spacing on raised beds inside the polyhouse with drip irrigation
Regular bending down of unproductive shoots and harvesting of mature buds at tight-bud stage on 50–60 cm long stems
Carry out formative pruning and training during initial 3–4 months to develop a strong scaffold framework
Apply fertigation schedule with N-P-K and micronutrients through drip along with IPM for thrips, spider mites, and powdery mildew

options.text: Plant saplings → Formative pruning → Fertigation and IPM → Bending and harvest
Bending and harvest → Plant saplings → Fertigation and IPM → Formative pruning
Fertigation and IPM → Bending and harvest → Plant saplings → Formative pruning
Formative pruning → Fertigation and IPM → Bending and harvest → Plant saplings

explanation: Commercial polyhouse rose production sequence: (1) plant grafted rose saplings at 30×30 cm on raised beds with drip irrigation installed — gives approximately 10,000 plants per 1000 m² polyhouse; (2) perform formative pruning and training over the first 3–4 months — removing weak shoots, bending down non-productive ones, and establishing 3–4 main scaffold canes; (3) regular fertigation with customised N-P-K (usually 200:100:200 mg/L in recirculating solution) and continuous IPM for thrips (Thrips tabaci), spider mites (Tetranychus urticae), and powdery mildew (Sphaerotheca pannosa); (4) begin harvesting tight-bud stage flowers on 50–60 cm stems with concurrent bending of non-productive shoots to divert energy to new flowering shoots. First commercial harvest begins at 4–5 months; full production by 6–8 months.

elimination_hints.hint: Planting saplings is always the first action — nothing can precede the plant being in the ground.
Harvesting requires mature flower buds — the final step after months of management.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following steps in the correct sequence for commercial marigold cultivation from seed sowing to harvest of loose flowers in Indian field conditions during the kharif season.
items: Sow marigold seeds in raised nursery beds during June–July at a rate of 1–1.5 kg seed per hectare of main field
Transplant 30–40 day-old seedlings to the main field at 45×45 cm spacing after final land preparation
Carry out pinching at 30–40 days after transplanting to encourage branching and increase flower number
Harvest flowers when fully open with firm petals during cool morning hours, continuing pickings every 3–4 days for 45–60 days

options.text: Sow in nursery → Transplant to main field → Pinching for branching → Harvest flowers every 3–4 days
Transplant to main field → Sow in nursery → Harvest flowers → Pinching for branching
Pinching for branching → Harvest flowers → Sow in nursery → Transplant to main field
Harvest flowers → Pinching for branching → Transplant to main field → Sow in nursery

explanation: Marigold cultivation sequence: (1) sow seed in raised nursery beds during June–July for kharif planting; (2) transplant 30–40 day-old seedlings (10–15 cm height) to main field at 45×45 cm spacing (giving 49,000 plants/ha); (3) pinch off the terminal growing point at 30–40 days after transplanting to encourage lateral branching and increase flower yield; (4) harvest fully open flowers with firm petals in cool morning hours every 3–4 days. Picking continues for 45–60 days giving total yield of 10–15 tonnes per hectare. Picking during cool hours reduces post-harvest wilting.

elimination_hints.hint: Nursery sowing must precede transplanting — seedlings must exist first.
Harvesting is the final economic step once flowers mature.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate whether the following statement is true or false: The International Flower Auction Bangalore (IFAB), established in 2002 with support from the Dutch flower industry, uses the descending-price Dutch auction system to sell cut flowers from Indian growers to domestic and export buyers.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This statement is true. The International Flower Auction Bangalore (IFAB) was established in 2002 as India's first electronic flower auction with technical support from the Dutch flower trading industry. It operates on the Dutch descending-price auction system — prices start high on an electronic clock and decrease until the first buyer presses a button to close the deal. IFAB auctions cut flowers (rose, gerbera, carnation, lily, orchid) from registered growers across Karnataka and nearby states to both domestic retailers and exporters. It has been a critical institution for modernising Indian cut flower trade.

elimination_hints.hint: IFAB's Dutch auction system and 2002 establishment are widely documented facts.
The Netherlands-India cooperation in flower auction modernisation is well-known.

topic: Floriculture: Gladiolus, Rose, Chrysanthemum, Marigold and Canna
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Study the diagram showing major flower crops of India along with their leading states, popular varieties, propagation methods, and commercial category, then identify the correct crop-state-variety-category combination.

options.text: Jasmine — Tamil Nadu — Madurai Malli (Jasminum sambac) — loose flower category
Rose — West Bengal — White Prosperity — loose flower for garlands
Marigold — Kerala — First Prize — cut flower for polyhouse export
Gladiolus — Tamil Nadu — Pusa Narangi Gainda — bulb flower for loose trade

explanation: From the diagram, jasmine is correctly identified with Tamil Nadu (leading state), Madurai Malli/Jasminum sambac (famous variety with GI tag), and loose flower category (used for hair decoration and garlands). Rose's leading state is Karnataka (not WB), White Prosperity is a gladiolus variety (not rose), and roses are primarily cut flowers (not loose). Marigold's leading state is Karnataka/Tamil Nadu (not Kerala), First Prize is a rose variety (not marigold), and marigold is a loose flower (not cut). Gladiolus is propagated by corms (not bulbs), White Prosperity is a gladiolus variety but Pusa Narangi Gainda is a marigold variety (not gladiolus), and gladiolus is a cut flower (not loose). Only option A correctly aligns all attributes.

elimination_hints.hint: White Prosperity is a gladiolus variety — rules out B.
First Prize is a rose variety — rules out C.
